summary: The dashboard is well-structured, correctly membership-scoped, and free of security or correctness-breaking defects (verified all referenced model enums/methods exist and the sweep's capture-before-update ordering is sound). Two performance findings, however, sit on hot paths and scale poorly: the recent-activity feed loads full unbounded history before trimming to 15 in Ruby, and the dashboard broadcast recomputes the entire active-pipeline tree plus fleet health for every project member on every worker progress tick. Two minor consistency/redundancy issues round it out.

findings.issue: Write-amplification on a hot path. StepRuns::BroadcastCard now calls Dashboard::Broadcast for every card update, including StepRuns::RecordProgress which fires on every worker progress tick. Dashboard::Broadcast#broadcast_summary recomputes Dashboard::ActivePipelines.new(user).call (which eager-loads phases->workflows->steps->step_runs for all of the user's active pipelines) and Dashboard::FleetHealth for EACH project member, then renders and pushes per member. A pipeline emitting frequent progress with several project members will re-run the full active-pipeline aggregation many times per second. Consider debouncing/throttling dashboard broadcasts, gating them to state-changing transitions rather than progress ticks, or moving the recompute off the request path.

Unbounded reads that grow with total history. Each of approval_events, rework_events, manager_events, and step_completion_events runs a query with NO database-level LIMIT or time window, materializes every matching row into Event structs, concatenates all four, then sorts in Ruby and keeps only first(15). step_completion_events in particular (StepRun where state in succeeded/failed) accumulates with every completed run for the lifetime of every project, so the working set — and the memory/CPU per dashboard load — grows without bound. This is also recomputed for every member on each activity broadcast (Dashboard::Broadcast#broadcast_activity). Bound each source query (e.g. ORDER BY the timestamp DESC LIMIT 15 at the DB, or a created_at window) before merging.

Asymmetric broadcasting leaves stale attention state. refresh_stuck_state broadcasts newly-stuck pipelines (broadcast_newly_stuck) but not the pipelines whose step_runs it just flipped stuck->ready via the `unstuck` update_all. A pipeline recovering from stuck therefore keeps its red 'Stuck' attention row/summary count on the dashboard until some other event (typically the subsequent Claim) triggers a broadcast. It self-heals on the next claim, but a recovered-yet-unclaimed run (e.g. role available but at capacity) can linger visibly stale. Capture and broadcast the unstuck pipeline ids the same way as the stuck ones.

Redundant duplicate fan-out. `@affected_decisions.each { Dashboard::Broadcast.call(pipeline: @phase.pipeline, activity: true) }` ignores the iteration variable and fires the identical full per-member dashboard fan-out once per recorded consensus/escalate decision. When a tick records more than one such decision, the same broadcast (same pipeline) is sent multiple times, multiplying the F1 recompute cost for no added information. Guard with a single `Dashboard::Broadcast.call(...) if @affected_decisions.any?`.
